content_en:
The salah chapter in Nihayat al-Muhtaj contains ar-Ramli's definitive statements on the Shafi'i law of prayer. His authority on these questions — combined with the complementary authority of Ibn Hajar al-Haytami in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj — gives scholars a reliable reference for navigating the complex landscape of Shafi'i prayer rulings.

Ar-Ramli confirms the seventeen pillars of salah as enumerated by an-Nawawi, with extensive analysis of each. On the opening takbir (Allahu Akbar), he explains that the takbir must be pronounced in Arabic with precisely this wording — no substitution is valid even if the substitute phrase praises Allah in stronger terms. This is a Shafi'i position derived from the Prophet's consistent practice and his statement 'prayer is entered by the takbir and exited by the salam.'

On the question of raising the hands (raf' al-yadayn), ar-Ramli confirms the Shafi'i sunnah of raising the hands at four points in the prayer: the opening takbir, the beginning of ruku', rising from ruku', and standing up for the third rak'ah after the first tashahhud. He explains that raising the hands at these four points is established by the hadith of Ibn Umar in Sahih al-Bukhari. He notes that the Hanafi school's restriction of raising hands to the opening takbir only represents a different weighing of the evidence.

The recitation of Surah al-Fatiha receives particular attention. Ar-Ramli confirms that it is obligatory in every rak'ah and discusses the technical requirements: the basmala is the first verse of al-Fatiha and must be recited as part of it; the prayer-specific form (Maliki al-yawm ad-din, not Maaliki al-yawm ad-din) must be observed; and the verses must be recited in order without any significant pause. He also addresses the obligation of reciting al-Fatiha in the second rak'ah even if the first rak'ah's recitation was long and the second short — the pillar applies to every rak'ah independently.

Ar-Ramli's discussion of the prayer of fear (salat al-khawf) — the prayer performed in conditions of imminent danger or battle — demonstrates the practical dimension of his legal analysis. He works through the several methods that the Prophet demonstrated for this prayer (described in multiple hadiths that are not all easily reconcilable), determining which are authentically established and how they are to be applied in different tactical situations.

The chapter on the prayer of the sick (salat al-marid) is practically important. Ar-Ramli explains the gradations: a person who can pray standing must do so; if unable, they may sit; if unable to sit, they lie on their right side facing the qiblah; if unable, on their back with feet toward the qiblah. At each stage, they perform the pillars as able: ruku' and sujud by inclining, or if even that is impossible, by gesturing with the eyes. The principle is that prayer is never dropped for an adult Muslim of sound mind.

Ar-Ramli addresses the Shafi'i school's qunoot in Fajr prayer: it is a confirmed sunnah (sunnah mu'akkadah), recited in the second rak'ah after rising from ruku'. He quotes the various authentic supplication texts and notes that the standard Du'a al-Qunoot attributed to al-Hasan ibn Ali is the most widely used. If a person forgets the qunoot, they perform the prostration of forgetfulness (sujud as-sahw) after the final tashahhud before the salam — following the Shafi'i rule that omitting a confirmed sunnah of prayer warrants this remedial prostration.
